statement: The area of polygon ABCDEF, In square units, is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The area is greater than 6 \times 5=30 and less than 6 \times 9=54 so (C) must be correct. \textbf{OR} Extending FE partitions the polygon into a rectangle and a square whose areas are 30 and 16 respectively. \textbf{OR} Extending AF and DC to form the large rectangle shows that area is (6 \times 9)-(4 \times 2)=54-8=46.

statement: The bar graph shows the grades in a mathematics class for the last grading period. If \text{A, B, C} and \text{D} are satisfactory grades, what fraction of the grades shown in the graph are satisfactory?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: By reading the graph, there are 5 \mathrm{~A}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}, 4 \mathrm{~B}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}, 3 \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}, 3 \mathrm{D}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}, and 5 \mathrm{F}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}. Thus the fraction of satisfactory grades is \dfrac{5+4+3+3}{20} = \dfrac{15}{20} = \dfrac{3}{4}. \textbf{OR} By reading the graph, \dfrac{5}{20}=\dfrac{1}{4} of the grades are not satisfactory so 1-\dfrac{1}{4}=\dfrac{3}{4} of the grades are satisfactory.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 5. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-5. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A "stair-step" figure 1 s made up of alternating black and white squares in each row. Rows 1 through 4 are shown. All rows begin and end with a white square. The number of black squares in the 37 th row is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The number of black squares is one less than the number of the row, so the 37 th row contains 36 black squares.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: If a=-2, the set is \{6,-8,-12,4,1\} so 6=3 \mathrm{a} is the largest. Notice that 4 a and \dfrac{24}{a} could be eliminated immediately since they are negative if a is negative.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: The desired product equals \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{3}{4} \times \ldots \times \dfrac{8}{9} \times \dfrac{9}{10}=\dfrac{1}{10} Notice that since \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{3}=\dfrac{1}{3}, the product is less than \dfrac{1}{3} so (C), (D) and (E) are easily eliminated.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

statement: If you walk for 45 minutes at a rate of 4 \mathrm{mph} and then run for 30 minutes at a rate of, 10 \mathrm{mph}, how many miles have you gone at the end of one hour and 15 minutes?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: To keep the units of miles and hours, first note 45 minutes =\dfrac{45}{60}=\dfrac{3}{4} hour and 30 minutes =\dfrac{1}{2} hour. Since distance = rate \times time, your total distance is 4 \times \dfrac{3}{4}+10 \times \dfrac{1}{2}=3+5=8 \text { miles. } The distance is less than 4+10=14 miles, so (D) and (E) can be easily eliminated.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 13. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-13. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: The difference is.5 \% of \$ 20=.005 \times \$ 20=\$.10.

statement: How many whole numbers between 100 and 400 contain the digit 2?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: In addition to the 100 numbers from 200-299, there are 20 numbers ending in 2 (e.g., 112, 342 ) and 20 numbers with a ten's digit of 2 (e.g., 127, 325 ). But the numbers 122 and 322 are counted twice in this process, so there are a total of 100+20+20-2=138.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 15. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-15. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The ratio of boys to girls in Mr. Brown's math class is 2:3. If there are 30 students in the class, how many more girls than boys are in the class?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: Since the ratio is 2: 3, \dfrac{2}{5} of the students are boys and \dfrac{3}{5} of them are girls. Thus there are \dfrac{1}{5} more girls than boys and \dfrac{1}{5} \times 30=6.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 16. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-16. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: If your average score on your first six mathematics tests was 84 and your average score on your first seven mathematics tests was 85, then your score on the seventh test was

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: To get an average of 85 on 7 tests, you needed a total of 7 \times 85=595 points. After 6 tests, you had a total of 6 \times 84=504 points. Thus you needed 595-504=91 points on the seventh test. \textbf{OR} If n was your score on the seventh test, then \dfrac{6(84)+n}{7}=85 so \mathrm{n}=91. \textbf{OR} To raise your average by one point, you needed seven additional points on the seventh test, so your score was 84+7=91.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 17. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-17. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Nine copies of a certain pamphlet cost less than \$ 10.00 while ten coples of the same pamphlet (at the same price) cost more than \$ 11.00. How much does one copy of this pamphlet cost?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: Only (E) satisfies the hypothesis that ten copies of the pamphlet cost more that \$ 11.00. \textbf{OR} If \mathrm{P} is the price of the pamphlet, then 9 \mathrm{P}<10 and 10P >11 or 1.10<\mathrm{P}<1.1111 \ldots. Thus P=\$ 1.11.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 18. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-18. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: If 2(\ell+w) is the original perimeter, then the new perimeter is 2(1.1 \ell+1.1 \mathrm{w})=2.2(\ell+\mathrm{w}) which is 10 \% more than 2(\ell+w).

statement: In a certain year, January had exactly four Tuesdays and four Saturdays. On what day did January 1 fall that year?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: January has 31 days. Had January 1 fallen on a Monday or Tuesday, then there would have been five Tuesdays - 2,9,16, 23,30 or 1,8,15,22,29. Likewise, had January 1 fallen on a Friday or Saturday, there would have been five Saturdays. Thus (C) is correct.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 20. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-20. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Mr. Green recelves a 10 \% raise every year. His salary after four such raises has gone up by: what percent?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: If the initial salary is thought of as \$ 100 then the first 10 \% increase gives \$ 110. The second 10 \% increase gives \$ 110+\$ 11=\$ 121. The third increase gives \$ 121+\$ 12.10=\$ 133.10 and the fourth increase gives \$ 133.10+\$ 13.31=\$ 146.41 for an increase of 46.41 \%. \textbf{OR} If S is the initial salary then the salary after four 10 \% increases is (1.1)(1.1)(1.1)(1.1)S =1.4641 S for an Increase of 46.41 \%.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 21. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-21. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Assume every 7 -digit whole number is a possible telephone number except those which begin with 0 or 1. What fraction of telephone numbers begin with 9 and end with 0?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: There are 10 digits. Excluding 0 and 1 leaves 8 digits. Thus \dfrac{1}{8} of all telephone numbers begin with 9. Of these, \dfrac{1}{10} end with 0 giving \dfrac{1}{8} \times \dfrac{1}{10}=\dfrac{1}{80} which begin with 9 and end with 0.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 22. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-22. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: King Middle School has 1200 students. Each student takes 5 classes a day. Each teacher teaches 4 classes. Each class has 30 students and 1 teacher. How many teachers are there at King Middle School?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: There are 1200 \times 5=6000 times a day a student attends a class. Thus there are \dfrac{6000}{30}=200 times a day a teacher teaches a class, so there must be \dfrac{200}{4}=50 teachers. \textbf{OR} If each student took 4 classes and each teacher taught 4 classes, then \dfrac{1200}{30}=40 teachers would be required. But each student takes 5 classes, so \dfrac{5}{4} \times 40=50 teachers are needed.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 23. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-23. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: In a magic trlangle, each of the six whole numbers 10 - 15 is placed in one of the circles so that the sum, S, of the three numbers on each side of the triangle is the same. The largest possible value for S is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: If the sum S is as large as possible. then the three largest numbers should be at the vertices so they each occur in two sums S. Thus S=\dfrac{2(13+14+15)+10+11+12}{3}=39. One such triangle is indicated.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 24. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-24. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Five cards are lying on a table as shown. Each card has a letter on one side and a whole number on the other side. Jane said, "If a vowel is on one side of any card, then an even number is on the other side." Mary showed Jane was wrong by turning over one card. Which card did Mary turn over?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: If Jane is wrong, then there is a card with a vowel on one side and an odd number on the other side. Such a card cannot have a consonant or an even number on either side. Thus the only card which could prove Jane wrong is the one with a " 3 " on one side. The other side would have to be a vowel. \textbf{OR} The easiest way for Mary to show Jane was wrong is for her to turn over a card showing a vowel, but there is no such card. But if Jane were correct, then so is the contrapositive: "If an odd number is on one side of a card, then a consonant is on the other side." Thus, Mary showed Jane was wrong by turning over the card marked with a 3.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 25. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-25. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A ream of paper containing 500 sheets is 5 \mathrm{~cm} thick. Approximately how many sheets of this type of paper would there be in a stack 7.5 \mathrm{~cm} high?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: The 7.5 \mathrm{~cm} stack is "half agaln" as tall as the 5 \mathrm{~cm} stack, so it will contain 500+\dfrac{1}{2}(500)=500+250=750 sheets. \textbf{OR} If \mathrm{n} is the number of sheets of paper in the 7.5 \mathrm{~cm} stack, then \dfrac{5}{500}=\dfrac{7.5}{n}. Thus n=750 sheets.
notes: Original paper position: 1985 AMC8 Problem 6. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1985/problem-6. Auto-gradable from verified answer and choices.

statement: In July 1861,366 inches of rain fell in Cherrapunji, India. What was the average rainfall in inches per hour during that month?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: The average rainfall per hour equals the total rainfall divided by the total number of hours. Since July has 31 days, the average is \dfrac{366}{31 \times 24} inches per hour.

solution: A large positive number has a small reciprocal and vice-versa. The smallest positive number \dfrac{1}{3} has the largest reciprocal, \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{3}}=3.

statement: The table to the right displays the grade distribution of the 30 students in a mathematics class on the last two tests. For example, exactly one student received a ' D ' on Test 1 and a ' C ' on Test 2 (see circled entry). What percent of the students received the same grade on both tests?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: A student received the same grade on both tests if s/he is counted on the main diagonal (from the top left to the bottom right) of the table. Thus the number of students receiving the same grade on both tests is 2+4+5+1+0=12. Consequently \dfrac{12}{30}=\dfrac{4}{10}=40 \% of the students received the same grade on both tests.

statement: Sale prices at the Ajax Outlet Store are 50 \% below original prices. On Saturdays an additional discount of 20 \% off the sale price is given. What is the Saturday price of a coat whose original price is \$ 180?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: The sale price of the coat is 50 \% of \$ 180 or \$ 90. The additional discount is 20 \% of \$ 90 or \$ 18, so the Saturday price is \$ 90-\$ 18=\$ 72. \textbf{OR} The Saturday price is 80 \% of the sale price and the sale price is 50 \% of the original price, so the Saturday price is.8(.5(\$ 180))=.8(\$ 90)=\$ 72.
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 15. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-15. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A bar graph shows the number of hamburgers sold by a fast food chain each season. However, the bar indicating the number sold during the winter is covered by a smudge. If exactly 25 \% of the chain's hamburgers are sold in the fall, how many million hamburgers are sold in the winter?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: If the fall sales of 4 million hamburgers are 25 \% of the yearly sales, then the yearly sales are 16 million hamburgers. Thus the winter sales are 16-(4.5+5+4)=2.5 million.
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 16. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-16. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Let o be an odd whole number and let n be any whole number. Which of the following statements about the whole number (o^{2}+ no) Is always true?
answer: it is odd only if n is even

choices.C: it is even onIy if n is even
choices.D: it is odd only if n is odd
choices.E: it is odd only if n is even

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Expressions and Equations

solution: The number o^{2} is always odd. Now (no) is odd if n is odd and even if n is even. Thus the sum (o^{2}+ no) is even if n is odd, since the sum of two odd numbers is even; the sum is odd if n is even, since the sum of an odd number and an even number is odd.
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 17. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-17. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A rectangular grazing area is to be fenced off on three sides using part of a 100 meter rock wall as the fourth side. Fence posts are to be placed every 12 meters along the fence including the two posts where the fence meets the rock wall. What is the fewest number of posts required to fence an area 36 \mathrm{~m} by 60 \mathrm{~m}?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The fewest number of posts is used if the wall serves as the longer side of the rectangular grazing area. Thus there are 6 posts on the 60 meter side (including the corners) and 3 more posts on each 36 meter side for a total of 12 posts.
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 18. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-18. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: At the beginning of a trip, the mileage odometer read 56,200 miles. The driver filled the gas tank wtth 6 gallons of gasoline. During the trip, the driver filled his tank again with 12 gallons of gasoline when the odometer read 56,560. At the end of the trip, the driver filled the tank agaln with 20 gallons of gasoline. The odometer read 57,060. To the nearest tenth, what was the car's average miles-per-gallon for the entire trip?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: The trip was 57,060-56,200=860 \mathrm{miles} long and 12+20=32 gallons of gasoline were used during the trip. Thus the average number of miles per gallon was \dfrac{860}{32}=26.9. Note that the 6 gallons needed to fill the tank at the start of the trip had no effect on the answer. No matter how many gallons were needed to fill the tank at the start, the average miles per gallon for the trip would be 26.9.
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 19. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-19. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The value of the expression \dfrac{(304)^{5}}{(29.7)(399)^{4}} is closest to

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Expressions and Equations

solution: Estimate each of the quantities to obtain the approximation: \begin{aligned} \dfrac{(300)^{5}}{30(400)^{4}} &= \dfrac{300}{30}\left(\dfrac{300}{400}\right)^{4} \\ &= 10\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^{4} \\ &= 10\left(\dfrac{81}{256}\right) \\ &\approx 10\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right) \approx 3. \end{aligned}
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 20. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-20. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Suppose one of the eight lettered identical squares is included with the four squares in ' the T-shaped figure outlined. How many of the resulting figures can be folded into a topless cubical box?

solution: Label the four squares in the T-shaped figure X, Y, Z, W as shown. First think of W as the base of the cube. statement: Alan, Beth, Carlos, and Diana were discussIng their possible grades in mathematics class this grading period. Alan said, "If I get an A, then Beth will get an A." Beth said, "If I get an A, then Carlos will get an A." Carlos said, "If I get an A, then Diana will get an A." All of these statements were true, but only two of the students received an A. Which two receıved A 's?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: If Alan received an " A ", then all the others would have received an " A ". If Beth received an " A ", then Carlos and Diana would also have received an " A ". Thus only Carlos and Diana received an " A ".

statement: The large circle has diameter AC. The two small circles have their centers on AC and just touch at 0, the center of the large circle. If each small circle has radius 1, what is the value of the ratio of the area of the shaded region to the area of one of the small circles?

choices.E: cannot be determined from the information given

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The radius of the large circle is 2 since it is a diameter of a small circle. The area of the large circle is \pi(2)^{2}=4 \pi and the area of each small circle is \pi. The shaded area is (by symmetry) half the difference of the areas of the large circle and the two small. circles, i.e. \dfrac{1}{2}(4 \pi-2 \pi)=\pi. Thus the desired ratio is 1.
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 23. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-23. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The 600 students at King Middle School are divided into three groups of equal size for lunch. Each group has lunch at a different time. A computer randomly assigns each student to one of the three lunch groups. The probability that three friends, Al, Bob, and Carol, will be assigned to the same lunch group is approximately

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: Al must be assigned to one of the lunch groups. The probability that Bob is assigned to the same Iunch group is approximately \dfrac{1}{3} ( \dfrac{199}{599} exactly) and the probability that Carol is assigned to that same group is also approximately \dfrac{1}{3} \left(\dfrac{198}{598}\right). Thus the probability that all three are assigned to the same group is approximately \dfrac{1}{3} \times \dfrac{1}{3}=\dfrac{1}{9}.
notes: Original paper position: 1986 AMC8 Problem 24. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1986/problem-24. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Which of the following sets of whole numbers has the largest average?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: In a set of whole numbers which are equally spaced, the average of the numbers in the set is the average of the smallest number and the largest number. For example, the average of \{1,3,5,7,9\}=\dfrac{1+9}{2}=5, and the average of \{2,5,8,11,14,17\} is \dfrac{2+17}{2}=9.5. In this problem, then, the averages are: \begin{aligned} \text{A:} \quad &\dfrac{2+100}{2}=51, \quad \text{B:} \quad \dfrac{3+99}{2}=51, \\ \text{C:} \quad &\dfrac{4+100}{2}=52, \quad \text{D:} \quad \dfrac{5+100}{2}=52.5, \\ \text{E:} \quad &\dfrac{6+96}{2}=51. \end{aligned} One could "guesstimate" that the set with the "largest" numbers should have the largest average. The numbers 5 and 100 are (overall) larger than the corresponding numbers in the other sets.

choices.E: depends on the values of A and B

solution: 7+3+B>9, so we "carry 1 " from the ten's column to the hundred's column. Similarly 1+8+\mathrm{A}>9 since \mathrm{A}>0, so we "carry 1 " from the hundred's place to the thousand's place. 1+9=10, so the sum is a 5 -digit number of the form 10 C D 9. \textbf{OR} Since A is a digit from 1 to 9, the sum 9876+ A32 must be between 10,000 and 100,000. statement: The sale ad read: "Buy three tires ar the regular price and get the fourth tire for \$ 3." Sam paid \$ 240 for a set of four tires at the sale. What was the regular price of one tire?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

statement: Joyce made 12 of her first 30 shots in the first three games of this basketball season, so her seasonal shooting average was 40 \%. In her next game, she took 10 shots and raised her seasonal shooting average to 50 \%. How many of these 10 shots did she make?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: In order to have a seasonal shooting average of 50 \% when having attempted 30+10=40 shots, Joyce must have made 20 of them. Thus she made 8 of her 10 shots in the next game.

solution: Since Bret is in seat \#3 and statement (1) is false, Carl must be in seat \#1. Then, since statement (2) is false, Abby must be in seat \#4 leaving, Dana in seat \#2.

statement: Suppose n * means \dfrac{1}{n}, the reciprocal of n. For example, 5 *=\dfrac{1}{5}. How many of the following statements are true? (i) \quad 3 * + 6 * = 9 * \\ (ii) \quad 6 * - 4 * = 2 * \\ (iii) \quad 2 * - 6 * = 12 * \\ (iv) \quad 10 * \div 2 * = 5 *

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: Statements i and ii are false; iii and iv are true. \begin{aligned} i. \quad &3 * + 6 * = \dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{1}{6} \neq \dfrac{1}{9} \\ iii. \quad &2 * \cdot 6 * = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{12} = 12 * \end{aligned}

statement: A B C D is a rectangle, D is the center of the circle, and B is on the circle. If A D=4 and C D=3, then the area of the shaded region is between

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: By the Pythagorean Theorem, A C=5. Since A C=B D, the radius of the circle is 5. The area of the shaded region, then, is \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \pi \cdot 5^{2}-3 \cdot 4 (a quarter circle with a rectangle deleted) =\dfrac{25 \pi}{4}-12. This quantity is clearly greater than \dfrac{76}{4}-12=7 and less than \dfrac{25\left(3 \dfrac{1}{5}\right)}{4}-12=8 since 3<\pi<3 \dfrac{1}{5}.

statement: Assume the adjoining chart shows the 1980 U.S. population, in millions, for each region by ethnic group. To the nearest percent, what percent of the U.S. Black population lived in the South? \begin{array}{l|cccc} & \mathrm{NE} & \mathrm{MW} & \text{South} & \text{West} \\ \hline \text{White} & 42 & 52 & 57 & 35 \\ \text{Black} & 5 & 5 & 15 & 2 \\ \text{Asian} & 1 & 1 & 1 & 3 \\ \text{Other} & 1 & 1 & 2 & 4 \\ \end{array}

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: The total Black population is the sum of the Black populations in each of the four regions or 5+5+15+2=27 million. Thus \dfrac{15}{27}=\dfrac{5}{9} \approx 55.56 \% lived in the South. (The population figures were taken from the World Almanac and adjusted slightly for convenience.)

statement: A multiple choice examination consists of 20 questions. The scoring is +5 for each correct answer, -2 for each incorrect answer, and 0 for each unanswered question. John's score on the examination is 48. What is the maximum number of questions he could have answered correctly?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: If John answered 13 or: more questions correctly, then his score would have been at least 13(5)-7(2)=51 ( 13 correct, 7 incorrect). Checking the other cases, we find that John could have answered 12. correctly, 6 incorrectly, and left 2 unanswered for a score of 12(5)-6(2)=48. Note that 10 correct, 1 incorrect, 9 unanswered also give a score of 48.

statement: Ten balls numbered 1 to 10 are in a jar. Jack reaches into the jar and randomly removes one of the balls. Then Jill reaches into the jar and randomly removes a different ball. The probability that the sum of the two numbers on the balls removed is even is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: Since Jack and Jill cannot remove the same number, there are 10 \cdot 9=90 ways they can remove the two balls from the jar as shown by the unshaded squares on the grid. Those squares representing an even sum are labeled " E ". There are 40 such squares - 4 in each column (or row) since the two numbers must both be odd or both be even. The probability is \dfrac{40}{90}=\dfrac{4}{9}. \textbf{OR} There are 10 ways to select the first number but only 4 ways to select the second since it must have the same parity (both odd or both even) as the first. Thus the probability is \dfrac{10 \cdot 4}{10 \cdot 9}=\dfrac{4}{9}.
notes: Original paper position: 1987 AMC8 Problem 25. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1987/problem-25. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The average weight of 6 boys is 150 pounds and the average weight of 4 girls is 120 pounds. The average weight of the 10 children is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: The total weight of the ten children is 6(150)+4(120)=1380, so the average weight is \dfrac{1380}{10} or 138 pounds. \textbf{OR} The average weight of 4 boys and 4 girls is 135 pounds. The other two boys would raise this average by \dfrac{30}{10} or 3 pounds.
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 18. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-18. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: What is the 100 th number in the arithmetic sequence: 1,5,9,13,17,21,25, \ldots?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: Adding 3 to each term in the original arithmetic sequence yields the sequence 4,8,12,16,20, \ldots in which the one-hundredth term is 400. Subtracting 3 from each term shows that 397 is the one-hundredth term of the original sequence. \textbf{OR} We may obtain the 100 th term in the sequence by adding 4 to the first term 1 a total of 99 times. Thus the $100 $th term is 1+99(4)=397.
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 19. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-19. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The glass gauge on a cylindrical coffee maker shows there are 45 cups left when the coffee maker is 36 \% full. How many cups of coffee does it hold when it is full?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: If n is the number of cups of coffee the coffeemaker will hold when it is full,then we can label the gauge as shown and write the two equivalent ratios: \dfrac{45}{n}=\dfrac{36}{100} \text{ or } n=125 \text{ cups}. \textbf{OR} If 36 \% is 45 \text{ cups}, then 4 \% is \dfrac{1}{9} (45) or 5 cups so that 100 \% is 25(5) or 125 cups.
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 20. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-20. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A fifth number, n, is added to the set of numbers \{3,6,9,10\} to make the mean of the set of five numbers equal to its median. The number of possible values for n is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: There are three possible values for the new number, n It is either less than 6, between 6 and 9, or greater than 9. If the five elements are listed in increasing order, these three possibilities in the table below are: one of the first two, the middle one, and one of the last two. Since the median is the middle number in a set of five elements, there are three values for the median: 6, n, or 9. \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline n & 3 & 6 & 9 & 10 & 6 \\ \hline 3 & n & 6 & 9 & 10 & 6 \\ \hline 3 & 6 & n & 9 & 10 & n \\ \hline 3 & 6 & 9 & n & 10 & 9 \\ \hline 3 & 6 & 9 & 10 & n & 9 \\ \hline \end{array} Query: Can you find the values of n and which of the two alternatives occurs in the first and third cases?
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 21. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-21. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Tom's Hat Shoppe increased all original prices by 25 \%. Now the shoppe is having a sale where all prices are 20 \% off these increased prices. Which statement best describes the sale price of an item?
answer: The sale price is the same as the original price.

choices.A: The sale price is 5 \% higher than the original price.
choices.B: The sale price is higher than the original price, but by less than 5 \%.
choices.C: The sale price is higher than the original price, but by more than 5 \%.
choices.D: The sale price is lower than the original price.
choices.E: The sale price is the same as the original price.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: An item whose original cost was \$ 100, for example, will cost \$ 25 more or \$ 125. The sale price of a \$ 125 item will be 80 \% of its current price or.8(\$ 125)=\$ 100 - - the original cost. The same kind of comparison can be made for any original cost.
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 22. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-22. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Maria buys computer disks at a price of 4 for \$ 5 and sells them at a price of 3 for \$ 5. How many computer disks must she sell in order to make a profit of \$ 100?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: Since the computer disks are bought in groups of 4 and sold in groups of 3, it is easier to consider them in groups of 12 or dozens. Each dozen costs \$ 15 and sells for \$ 20 giving a profit of \$ 5. Thus to get a profit of \$ 100, she must sell 20 dozen or 240 computer disks.
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 23. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-23. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The square in the first diagram "rolls" clockwise around the fixed regular hexagon until it reaches the bottom. In which position will the solid triangle be in diagram 4?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: Keep track of the "bottom" side of the square in the first figure. In the fourth figure, it will appear on the left, so the solid triangle will be in the position shown in (A). \textbf{OR} Each time the square "rolls" to the next edge of the hexagon, it turns through an angle of 150^{\circ}. In going from the top to the bottom of the hexagon, the square makes three such turns for a total of 3(150)=450^{\circ}. This 450^{\circ} represents one complete revolution and 1 / 4 of a second revolution.
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 24. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-24. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A palindrome is a whole number that reads the same forwards as backwards. If one neglects the colon, certain times displayed on a digital watch are palindromes. Three examples are: 1:01, 4: 44 and 12: 21. How many times during a 12 -hour period will be palindromes?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: In each hour from 1{:}00 through 9{:}59, there are six such times. From 3{:}00 to 3{:} 59, for example, these times are 3{:}03, 3{:}13, 3{:}23, 3{:}33, 3{:}43, 3{:}53. From 10{:}00 to 12{:}59, there is one such time in each hour: 10{:}01, 11{:}11, and 12{:} 21. Thus there are a total of 9(6)+3=57 such times.
notes: Original paper position: 1988 AMC8 Problem 25. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1988/problem-25. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: The sum is 5(50)=250 as can be seen by grouping the sum as shown. \textbf{OR} The sum is 10+30+50+70+90=250 as can be seen by grouping the sum as shown.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 1. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-1. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 2. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-2. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Which of the following numbers is the largest?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: We can compare the five numbers in the set by annexing zeroes so each one has four decimal places. The numbers, then, are.9900,.9099,.9000,.9090,.9009$ so that.99=.9900 is the largest.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 3. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-3. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Estimate to determine which of the following numbers is closest to \dfrac{401}{.205}.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: \dfrac{401}{.205} \approx \dfrac{400}{.2}=\dfrac{4000}{2}=2000 where \approx means "is approximately equal to". \textbf{OR} \dfrac{401}{.205}=\dfrac{1}{.205} \times 401 \approx 5 \times 400=2000.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 4. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-4. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: -15+9 \times(6 \div 3)=

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: Using the standard order of operations, \begin{aligned} &-15 + 9 \times (6 \div 3) \\ = &-15 + 9 \times 2 \quad &\text{[operate inside parentheses]} \\ = &-15 + 18 \quad &\text{[multiplication precedes addition]} \\ = & \ 3. \end{aligned}
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 5. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-5. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: If the markings on the number line are equally spaced, what is the number y?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: Since the distance between two consecutive marks is \dfrac{1}{5} of the total distance, y=3 \times\left(\dfrac{1}{5} \times 20\right)=3 \times 4=12. \textbf{OR} Use a proportion: \dfrac{y}{20}=\dfrac{3}{5}, so y=12.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 6. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-6. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: If the value of 20 quarters and 10 dimes equals the value of 10 quarters and n dimes, then n=

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: The value of 20 quarters and 10 dimes is \$ 5.00+\$ 1.00=\$ 6.00. Since the value of 10 quarters is \$ 2.50, the remaining \$ 3.50 must consist of 35 dimes. \textbf{OR} We can think of the 20-10=10 quarters as being "traded in" for dimes. The value of 10 quarters, \$ 2.50, is 25 dimes. Combining them with the original 10 dimes gives a total of 35 dimes.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 7. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-7. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: (2 \times 3 \times 4)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{1}{4}\right)=

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: Using the distributive law, the product equals \begin{aligned} &(2 \times 3 \times 4) \times\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)+(2 \times 3 \times 4) \times\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)+(2 \times 3 \times 4) \times\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right) \\ &= (3 \times 4) + (2 \times 4) + (2 \times 3) \\ &= 12 + 8 + 6 = 26. \end{aligned} Although it is more tedious, one can perform the operations within each set of parentheses to obtain: 24\left(\dfrac{6}{12}+\dfrac{4}{12}+\dfrac{3}{12}\right)=24\left(\dfrac{13}{12}\right)=26.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 8. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-8. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: There are 2 boys for every 3 girls in Ms. Johnson's math class. If there are 30 students in her class, what percent of them are boys?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: We see that 2 out of every 5 students or \dfrac{2}{5} or 40 \% are boys. Note: The number of studenis in the class is not needed to solve the problem.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 9. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-9. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: What is the number of degrees in the smaller angle between the hour hand and the minute hand on a clock that reads seven o'clock?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The numerals on a clock face divide it into twelve equal sections of 30^{\circ}. The smaller of the two regions determined by the hands at 7{:}00 p.m. covers five of these sections, so the desired angle is 150^{\circ}.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 10. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-10. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Which of the five "T-like shapes" would be symmetric to the one shown with respect to the dashed line?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: Two figures are symmetric with respect to a line if the figures coincide when the paper is folded along that line.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 11. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-11. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: \dfrac{1-\dfrac{1}{3}}{1-\dfrac{1}{2}}= \dfrac{\dfrac{2}{3}}{\dfrac{1}{2}} = \dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{2}{1} = \dfrac{4}{3}. \textbf{OR} Multiplying both numerator and denominator by 6 yields \dfrac{6\left(1-\dfrac{1}{3}\right)}{6\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}= \dfrac{6-2}{6-3} = \dfrac{4}{3}.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 12. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-12. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: When placing each of the digits 2,4,5,6,9 in exactly one of the boxes of this subtraction problem, what is the smallest difference that is possible?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: The smallest difference occurs when the minuend is as small as possible and the subtrahend is as large as possible. Thus 245-96=149 is the smallest difference.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 14. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-14. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The area of the shaded region BEDC in parallelogram \mathrm{ABCD} is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The shaded area is the difference of the area of the parallelogram and the area of the unshaded triangle. The area of the parallelogram is 10 \times 8=80. The base of the triangle is 10-6=4 and its height is 8, so its area is \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 8=16. Thus the shaded area is 80-16=64. \textbf{OR} The shaded region is a trapezoid with bases 10 and 6, and height 8. Its area is \dfrac{1}{2}(8)(10+6)=64.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 15. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-15. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: In how many ways can 47 be written as the sum of two primes?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: It is not possible to write 47 as the sum of two primes. When 47 is written as the sum of two whole numbers, one must be even and the other odd. Since 2 is the only even prime, there is only one case to consider. In that case, the other summand must be 45 which is not a prime since it has a factor of 5.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 16. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-16. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The number N is between 9 and 17. The average of 6,10, and N could be

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: Since \mathrm{N} is between 9 and 17, the average \dfrac{6+10+\mathrm{N}}{3} must lie between \dfrac{6+10+9}{3}=8 \dfrac{1}{3} and \dfrac{6+10+17}{3}=11. Only 10 lies in this range. \textbf{OR} The average of 6=10-4,10, and 14=10+4 is clearly 10. Since 9<14<17, a possible average is 10. Since the question has only one answer, it must be 10.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 17. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-17. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Many calculators have a reciprocal key \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{x}} that replaces the current number displayed with its reciprocal. For example, if the display is 4 and the \dfrac{1}{x} key is depressed, then the display becomes \square.25. If 32 is currently displayed, what is the fewest number of times you must depress the \left[\dfrac{1}{x}\right] key so the display again reads 32?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: Depressing the key one time yields the reciprocal of 32 or \dfrac{1}{32}. Thus depressing the key a second time yields the reciprocal of \dfrac{1}{32} or \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{32}}=32.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 18. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-18. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The graph below shows the total accumulated dollars (in millions) spent by the Surf City government during 1988. For example, about.5 million had been spent by the beginning of February and approximately 2 million by the end of April. Approximately how many millions of dollars were spent during the summer months of June, July, and August?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: The graph shows expenditures of a bit more than \$ 2 million by the beginning of June and a bit more than \$ 4.5 million by the end of August. Thus about 4.5-2.0=\$ 2.5 million was spent during the summer months.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 19. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-19. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The figure may be folded along the lines shown to form a number cube. Three number faces come together at each corner of the cube. What is the largest sum of three numbers whose faces come together at a comer?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: Each number will share a comer with every number on the cube except the one on the opposite face. Thus the combinations involving 1-3, 2-5, and 4-6 cannot occur. Consequently the largest sum is 6+5+3=14.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 20. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-20. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Jack had a bag of 128 apples. He sold 25 \% of them to Jill. Next he sold 25 \% of those remaining to June. Of those apples still in his bag, he gave the shiniest one to his teacher. How many apples did Jack have then?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: Since 25 \%=.25=\dfrac{1}{4}, Jill bought \dfrac{1}{4} \times 128=32 apples. June bought \dfrac{1}{4} of the remaining 128-32=96 apples or 24 apples. This leaves 72 apples -- one for the teacher and 71 for Jack. \textbf{OR} Keep track of the number of apples that Jack has at the end of each transaction: \begin{aligned} \text{Jill:} \quad &\dfrac{3}{4} \times 128 = 96 \\ \text{June:} \quad &\dfrac{3}{4} \times 96 = 72 \\ \text{Teacher:} \quad &72 - 1 = 71 \end{aligned}
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 21. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-21. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: The letters A,J,H,S,M,E and the digits 1,9,8,9 are "cycled" separately as follows and put in a numbered list: \begin{aligned} && AJHSME \ 1989 \\ 1.&\quad &JHSMEA \ 9891 \\ 2.&\quad &HSMEAJ \ 8919 \\ 3.&\quad &SMEAJH \ 9198 \\ &&.... \end{aligned} What is the number of the line on which AJHSME \ 1989 will appear for the first time?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: The 6 letters will go through a complete cycle every 6 lines and the 4 numbers every 4 lines. The letters and numbers together will go through a complete cycle every LCM (6,4)=12 times. Thus AJHSME 1989 occurs for the first time on the 12 th line.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 22. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-22. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: An artist has 14 cubes, each with an edge of 1 meter. She stands them on the ground to from a sculpture as shown. She then paints the exposed surface of the sculpture. How many square meters does she paint?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: There are 6 faces exposed on each of the four sides for a total of 6 \times 4=24 square meters. From above, there are a total of 9 square meters exposed since the cubes in the second and third layers essentially cover the unexposed portion of the bottom layer. So there are a total of 24+9=33 square meters to paint. \textbf{OR} The bottom layer has 12 complete faces, four " \dfrac{1}{2} faces", and four " \dfrac{3}{4} faces". The second layer has 8 complete faces and four " 3 4 faces". The top layer has 5 complete faces. The total, then, is 12+4\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)+4\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)+8+4\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)+5=33 \text{ square meters}.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 23. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-23. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Suppose a square piece of paper is folded in half vertically. The folded paper is then cut in half along the dashed line. Three rectangles are formed-a large one and two small ones. What is the ratio of the perimeter of one of the small rectangles to the perimeter of the large rectangle?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: If we let the side of the original square be 4 units, then the large rectangle and one of the small rectangles formed have the dimensions shown. Therefore the ratio of the perimeters is \dfrac{1+4+4+1}{2+4+4+2} = \dfrac{10}{12} = \dfrac{5}{6}.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 24. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-24. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Every time these two wheels are spun, two numbers are selected by the pointers. What is the probability that the sum of the two selected numbers is even?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: The set of all the possible outcomes of spinning each wheel is \{(5,6),(5,7),(5,9),(8,6),(8,7),(8,9),(4,6),(4,7),(4,9),(3,6),(3,7),(3,9)\} There are six even sums among the 12 outcomes for a probability of \dfrac{6}{12}=\dfrac{1}{2}. \textbf{OR} Consider the outcome on the right wheel first. Whatever number occurs, there are two out of four chances for the number on the left wheel to give an even sum. Thus the desired probability is \dfrac{2}{4}=\dfrac{1}{2}.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 25. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-25. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: \dfrac{9}{7 \times 53}=

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: To get.9 in the numerator, we must divide it by 10. To maintain equality, we must also divide the denominator by 10. Thus \dfrac{\dfrac{9}{10}}{\dfrac{7 \times 53}{10}} =\dfrac{\dfrac{9}{10}}{\dfrac{7}{10} \times 53}=\dfrac{.9}{.7 \times 53}. In all other cases, the resulting fraction is \dfrac{1}{10} or \dfrac{1}{100} of the original fraction.
notes: Original paper position: 1989 AMC8 Problem 13. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1989/problem-13. Auto-gradable from verified answer and choices.

statement: What is the smallest sum of two 3 -digit numbers that can be obtained by placing each of the six digits 4,5,6,7,8,9 in one of the six boxes in this addition problem?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: In the smallest such sum, the two smallest digits are in the hundred's places, the next two digits in the ten's places and the two largest digits are in the one's places. One example is 468+579=1047. Query: In how many ways can this sum of 1047 be achieved?

statement: Which digit of.12345, when changed to 9, gives the largest number?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: An increase in the tenth's place gives a larger value than an increase in any of the other decimal places. Since 1 is in the tenth's place of.12345, (A) is correct.

statement: What fraction of the square is shaded?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: Each shaded piece above or below the diagonal is matched by an identical unshaded piece meaning \dfrac{1}{2} of the total area is shaded.
notes: Original paper position: 1990 AMC8 Problem 3. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1990/problem-3. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Which of the following could not be the unit's digit [one's digit] of the square of a whole number?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The unit's digit of the square of a whole number is determined by the square of its unit's digit. The possible squares of unit digits are: \begin{aligned} 0^2 &= 0, \quad &1^2 &= 1, \quad &2^2 &= 4, \quad &3^2 &= 9, \quad &4^2 &= 16, \\ 5^2 &= 25, \quad &6^2 &= 36, \quad &7^2 &= 49, \quad &8^2 &= 64, \quad &9^2 &= 81 \end{aligned} Note that 2,3,7, or 8 will never occur as the unit's digit of a square.
notes: Original paper position: 1990 AMC8 Problem 4. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1990/problem-4. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Which of the following is closest to the product (.48017)(.48017)(.4801.7)?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: The desired product is about \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{1}{8}=0.125. \textbf{OR} The desired product is about (.5)(.5)(.5)=0.125.
notes: Original paper position: 1990 AMC8 Problem 5. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1990/problem-5. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Which of these five numbers is the largest?

solution: All of the choices, except (C) and (D), are near 13,579. In (D), the result is the product (13579)(2468) while in (C) the result is much less than 13,579.
