statement: Let \boxed{N} mean the number of whole number divisors of N. For example, 3=2, because 3 has two divisors, 1 and 3. Find the value of \boxed{\boxed{11} \times \boxed{20}}.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: Both 1 and 11 divide 11, so 11=2, and since 1,2,4,5,10, and 20 divide 20, then 20=6. The inner expression, 11 \times 20=2 \times 6=12. Finally, 12=6 because 1,2,3,4,6, and 12 divide 12.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 11. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-11. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: \angle 1+\angle 2=180^{\circ}. \angle 3=\angle 4. Find \angle 4.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: Since the sum of the angles of a triangle is 180^{\circ}, 40^{\circ}+70^{\circ}+\angle 1= 180^{\circ} and \angle 1=70^{\circ}. This means that \angle 2=110^{\circ}. Then 110^{\circ}+\angle 3+\angle 4=180^{\circ}, so \angle 3+\angle 4=70^{\circ} and \angle 3=\angle 4=35^{\circ}.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 12. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-12. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Three bags of jelly beans contain 26,28, and 30 beans. The ratios of yellow beans to all beans in each of these bags are 50 \%, 25 \%, and 20 \%, respectively. All three bags of candy are dumped into one bowl. Which of the following is closest to the ratio of yellow jelly beans to all beans in the bowl?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: There is a total of 26+28+30=84 jelly beans: \begin{alignedat}{2} & 50\% \text{ of } 26 = 13 \\ & 25\% \text{ of } 28 = 7 \\ & 20\% \text{ of } 30 = \frac{6}{26} \quad \frac{26}{84} = 0.3095 \approx 31\%. \end{alignedat}
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 13. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-13. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: There is a set of five positive integers whose average (mean) is 5, whose median is 5, and whose only mode is 8. What is the difference between the largest and smallest integers in the set?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: Since 5 is the median, there must be two integers greater than 5 and two less than 5. The five integers may be arranged this way: 5 8 8. Since the mean is 5, the sum of all five integers is 25. The numbers 5,8, and 8 total 21, leaving 4 for the sum of the first two. They can't both be 2 since 8 is the only mode, so they are 1 and 3. The difference between 8 and 1 is 7.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 14. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-14. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Each side of the large square in the figure is trisected (divided into three equal parts). The corners of an inscribed square are at these trisection points, as shown. The ratio of the area of the inscribed square to the area of the large square is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: Taking the side of the large square to be 3 inches gives an area of 9 square inches. Each of the four right triangles has an area of \dfrac{1}{2}(2)(1)=1 sq. inch. The area of the inscribed square is the area of the large square minus the area of the four right triangles, that is, 9-4(1)=5 sq. inches. The desired ratio is \dfrac{5}{9}.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 15. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-15. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Penni Precisely buys \$ 100 worth of stock in each of three companies: Alabama Almonds, Boston Beans, and California Cauliflower. After one year, AA was up 20 \%, BB was down 25 \%, and CC was unchanged. For the second year, AA was down 20 \% from the previous year, BB was up 25 \% from the previous year, and CC was unchanged. If A, B, and C are the final values of the stock, then

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: At the end of two years, stock values are: \begin{alignedat}{2} & \text{Stock } AA{:} \ && \$100(1.2)(0.8) = \$96 \\ & \text{Stock } BB{:} \ && \$100(0.75)(1.25) = \$93.75 \\ & \text{Stock } CC{:} \ && \$100(1)(1) = \$100 \end{alignedat} So, B<A<C.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 16. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-16. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A cube has eight vertices (corners) and twelve edges. A segment, such as \mathrm{x}, which joins two vertices not joined by an edge is called a diagonal. Segment y is also a diagonal. How many diagonals does a cube have?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: There are two diagonals, such as x, in each of the six faces for a total of twelve face diagonals. There are also four space diagonals, such as y, which are within the cube. This makes a total of 16.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 17. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-17. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: At the grocery store last week, small boxes of facial tissue were priced at 4 boxes for \$ 5. This week they are on sale at 5 boxes for \$ 4. The percent decrease in the price per box during the sale was closest to

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: Last week one box cost \$ 1.25; this week one box costs \$ 0.80. The decrease, \$ 0.45, compared to the original price of \$ 1.25, is a decrease of \dfrac{0.45}{1.25}=0.36 or 36 \%, so choice (B) is closest. \textbf{OR} Last week there was a 4 -box offer and this week a 5 -box offer. Consider a purchase of 20 boxes (the smallest number divisible by both 4 and 5). Last week 20 boxes would have been \$ 25 (5 offers). This week it is \$ 16 (4 offers). The savings, \$ 9, compared to \$ 25 is 36 \%.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 18. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-18. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: If the product \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{5}{4} \cdot \dfrac{6}{5} \cdot \ldots \cdot \dfrac{a}{b}=9, what is the sum of a and b?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: Since \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{5} \cdot \cdots \cdot \dfrac{a}{b}=9, we see that \dfrac{a}{2}=9. Thus, a=18, b=17, and a+b=35.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 19. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-19. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A pair of 8 -sided dice have sides numbered 1 through 8. Each side has the same probability (chance) of landing face up. The probability that the product of the two numbers on the sides that land face-up exceeds 36 is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Data, Counting, and Probability

solution: There are 64 equally likely possibilities for the numbers on the two dice. Of these, only (5,8), (6,7), (6,8), (7,6), (7,7), (7,8), (8,5), (8,6), (8,7), and (8,8) give products exceeding 36, so the probability of this occurring is \dfrac{10}{64}=\dfrac{5}{32}. \textbf{OR} Make a table for the sample space: \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline x & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ \hline 1 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\ \hline 2 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 & 12 & 14 & 16 \\ \hline 3 & 3 & 6 & 9 & 12 & 15 & 18 & 21 & 24 \\ \hline 4 & 4 & 8 & 12 & 16 & 20 & 24 & 28 & 32 \\ \hline 5 & 5 & 10 & 15 & 20 & 25 & 30 & 35 & (40) \\ \hline 6 & 6 & 12 & 18 & 24 & 30 & 36 & (42) & (48) \\ \hline 7 & 7 & 14 & 21 & 28 & 35 & (42) & (49) & (56) \\ \hline 8 & 8 & 16 & 24 & 32 & (40) & (48) & (56) & (64) \\ \hline \end{array} The ten circled products exceed 36 so the probability of this occurring is \dfrac{10}{64} or \dfrac{5}{32}.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 20. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-20. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: A two-inch cube (2 \times 2 \times 2) of silver weighs 3 pounds and is worth \$ 200. How much is a three-inch cube of silver worth?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: The volume of a two-inch cube is 2^3 = 8\,\mathrm{cu}.\,\mathrm{inches}, while that of a three-inch cube is 27\,\mathrm{cu}.\,\mathrm{inches}. Therefore, the weight and value of the larger cube is \dfrac{27}{8} times that of the smaller. \$200\left(\dfrac{27}{8}\right) = \$675. Note: The actual weight of the cubes is not needed to solve the problem.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 22. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-22. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: There are positive integers that have these properties: I. the sum of the squares of their digits is 50, and II. each digit is larger than the one to its left. The product of the digits of the largest integer with both properties is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: To meet the first condition, numbers which sum to 50 must be chosen from the set of squares \{1,4,9,16,25,36,49\}. To meet the second condition, the squares selected must be different. Consequently, there are three possibilities: 1+49,1+4+9+36, and 9+16+25. These correspond to the integers 17,1236, and 345, respectively. The largest is 1236, and the product of its digits is 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 6=36.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 23. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-23. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: ACE is divided at \mathrm{C} in the ratio 2: 3. The two semicircles, A B C and C D E, divide the circular region into an upper (shaded) region and a lower region. The ratio of the area of the upper region to that of the lower region is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: Let the diameter of the large circle equal 10. Then the ratio is: \frac{ \left( \text{Area of semicircle } R + S \right) - \left( \text{Area of semicircle } S \right) + \left( \text{Area of semicircle } T \right) }{ \left( \text{Area of semicircle } T + U \right) - \left( \text{Area of semicircle } T \right) + \left( \text{Area of semicircle } S \right) } = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \pi 5^{2} - \frac{1}{2} \pi 2^{2} + \frac{1}{2} \pi 3^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \pi 5^{2} - \frac{1}{2} \pi 3^{2} + 2^{2}} = \frac{15 \pi}{10 \pi} = \frac{3}{2} \text{ or } 3:2.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 24. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-24. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: All of the even numbers from 2 to 98 inclusive, except those ending in 0, are multiplied together. What is the rightmost digit (the units digit) of the product?

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Theory and Integer Structure

solution: If the numbers 2,4,6, and 8 are multiplied, the product is 384, so 4 is the final digit of the product of a set of numbers ending in 2,4,6, and 8. Since there are ten such sets of numbers, the final digit of the overall product is the same as the final digit of 4^{10}. Now, 4^{10}=\left(4^{2}\right)^{5}=16^{5}. Next, consider 6^{5}. Since any number of 6's multiply to give 6 as the final digit, the final digit of the required product is 6.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 25. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-25. Auto-gradable from extracted answer key.

statement: Each corner cube is removed from this 3 \mathrm{~cm} \times 3 \mathrm{~cm} \times 3 \mathrm{~cm} cube. The surface area of the remaining figure is

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Geometry and Measurement

solution: In terms of the original cube, three square centimeters are lost from each corner, but three new squares are added as the sides of the cavity in that corner. The total area remains at 54 square centimenters. \textbf{OR} Each face had an area of 9 square centimeters originally, so the total area was 6(9) = 54\,\mathrm{sq}.\,\mathrm{cm}. Four squares are lost from each face, leaving 54 - 4(6) = 30\,\mathrm{sq}.\,\mathrm{cm}. But the cavity in each of the eight corners has an area of 3\,\mathrm{sq}.\,\mathrm{cm}, so 8(3) = 24\,\mathrm{sq}.\,\mathrm{cm} must be added. Finally, 30 + 24 = 54\,\mathrm{sq}.\,\mathrm{cm}.
notes: Original paper position: 1997 AMC8 Problem 21. Verified from Random Math: https://wiki.randommath.com/amc8/1997/problem-21. Auto-gradable from verified answer and choices.

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Arithmetic and Proportional Reasoning

solution: First reduction: 10-0.2(10)=10-2=8. Second reduction: 8-0.5(8)=8-4=4. \textbf{OR} The sale price is 80 \% of the original price. After the next reduction, the final price is one-half the sale price, or one-half of 80 \% or 40 \%. Therefore, 0.4(10)=4.

solution: Since Harry has 3 sisters and 5 brothers, there are 3 girls and 6 boys in the family. So Harriet has 2 sisters and 6 brothers. The product of 2 and 6 is 12.

statement: 2\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)+3\left(1-\dfrac{1}{3}\right)+4\left(1-\dfrac{1}{4}\right)+\ldots+10\left(1-\dfrac{1}{10}\right)=

topic.chapterTitle: AMC8 Topic: Number Systems and Operations

solution: \begin{aligned} &2\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) + 3\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{3}\right) + 4\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{4}\right) + \ldots + 10\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{10}\right) \\ &= 2\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) + 3\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right) + 4\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) + \ldots + 10\left(\dfrac{9}{10}\right) \\ &= 1 + 2 + 3 + \ldots + 9 = 45. \end{aligned}
